schools.summary: Paul, turning ('finally') toward the close, sounds the keynote of the epistle: 'rejoice in the Lord' — joy not in circumstances but in the Lord himself, the abiding source of gladness; and his readiness to 'write the same things' (perhaps the warnings against the Judaizers, or the call to joy) is 'not grievous (not irksome)' to him 'but for you it is safe (a safeguard)' — repetition guards them from error (Barnes, Robertson).
Chrysostom (with Calvin): the believer's joy is grounded in Christ, independent of outward things; even from prison Paul bids them rejoice, for the Lord is the unfailing fountain of joy.

themes: Finally, my brethren, rejoice in the Lord
To write the same things to you... for you it is safe
the call to rejoice in the Lord
the safety of repeated warning

schools.summary: Paul's vehement, threefold 'beware' against the false teachers: 'beware of dogs' — the Judaizers, called 'dogs' (the term the Jews used for Gentiles, now turned on them as impudent, unclean, snapping at the truth); 'evil workers' — those who, though they labour, work evil, their activity in spreading legalism harming the church; 'the concision' (katatome — a mere cutting, mutilation) — Paul refuses them the honourable name 'circumcision' (peritome), calling their rite a mere fleshly mutilation, now that the true circumcision is of the heart (Barnes, Robertson, JFB, Wesley).

themes: Beware of dogs, beware of evil workers, beware of the concision
the threefold warning against the Judaizing teachers
dogs, evil workers, the concision
the cutting pun on circumcision

schools.summary: Paul's striking assertion: 'we (believers in Christ) are the circumcision' — the true circumcision is no longer the fleshly rite but the spiritual reality, the people of God marked inwardly by the Spirit (Romans 2:29); the threefold mark being that they 'worship God in the spirit (by the Spirit, spiritually)' — true worship inward not ritual; 'rejoice (glory, boast) in Christ Jesus' (not in the law or themselves); and 'have no confidence in the flesh' (in fleshly privileges, rites, or works) — Barnes, Robertson, Wesley.

Calvin: the true circumcision is spiritual — worship by the Spirit, glorying in Christ alone, and the renouncing of all confidence in the flesh; this marks the genuine people of God against the carnal trust of the Judaizers.

themes: we are the circumcision, which worship God in the spirit
rejoice in Christ Jesus, and have no confidence in the flesh
believers the true circumcision
worship in the Spirit, glorying in Christ

schools.summary: Paul anticipates an objection: he renounces confidence in the flesh not because he had none, for 'I might also have confidence in the flesh' — he possessed every fleshly advantage the Judaizers boasted; 'if any other man thinketh that he hath whereof he might trust in the flesh, I more' — whatever ground for fleshly confidence anyone could claim, Paul had more, no one able to outdo him in Jewish privilege (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: Paul's renunciation of fleshly confidence is the more striking because he had the most to renounce; he speaks not as one who had nothing, but as one who gave up everything for Christ.

themes: I might also have confidence in the flesh
If any other man thinketh that he hath whereof he might trust in the flesh, I more
Paul had more ground than any for fleshly confidence
the weight of his renunciation

schools.summary: Paul lists his fleshly credentials: 'circumcised the eighth day' (according to the law, not a proselyte circumcised later), 'of the stock (race) of Israel' (a true-born Israelite), 'of the tribe of Benjamin' (an honoured tribe, that of the first king and faithful in the division), 'an Hebrew of the Hebrews' (a pure-blooded Hebrew, of Hebrew parentage and speech); 'as touching the law, a Pharisee' (of the strictest sect, the most rigorous observers of the law) — every advantage of Jewish descent and of religious zeal (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: Circumcised the eighth day, of the stock of Israel, of the tribe of Benjamin, an Hebrew of the Hebrews
as touching the law, a Pharisee
Paul's pedigree of Jewish privilege
credentials of birth and sect

schools.summary: Paul's further credentials: 'concerning zeal, persecuting the church' — his religious zeal so intense that it drove him to persecute the church, by the standard of zealous Judaism a mark of devotion; 'touching the righteousness which is in the law (the outward, external righteousness of legal observance), blameless' — measured by the law's external requirements, no one could fault him, he kept its rules irreproachably (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: by every human and legal measure Paul was a model — yet all this he counted loss; the very righteousness men prized he renounced for Christ, showing that even the highest fleshly attainment is nothing before God.

themes: concerning zeal, persecuting the church
touching the righteousness which is in the law, blameless
Paul's zeal and legal blamelessness
the height of fleshly righteousness

schools.summary: The great reversal: 'what things were gain (advantages, things he had reckoned as profit — his pedigree, zeal, and legal righteousness) to me' — all that he once counted as spiritual capital — 'those I counted (have reckoned, and still reckon) loss for Christ' — he now regards all his former gains not merely as worthless but as positive loss, hindrances to be cast away for the sake of Christ (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): what once seemed Paul's greatest assets he now sees as liabilities, for they had stood between him and Christ; the gain became loss when set against the surpassing worth of Christ.

themes: what things were gain to me, those I counted loss for Christ
Paul counting his former gains as loss
the great reversal of conversion
gain become loss for Christ

schools.summary: Paul broadens the reckoning: not only his Jewish gains but 'all things' he counts loss 'for the excellency (the surpassing worth, the supreme value) of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord' — the knowing of Christ outweighs everything; he has actually 'suffered the loss of all things' (forfeited all his former advantages) and counts them 'but dung (skybala — refuse, filth, what is thrown to dogs)' — not merely worthless but loathsome, to be cast away with disgust, 'that I may win (gain, acquire) Christ' (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the whole purpose is to win Christ — to possess Christ himself is the prize for which all else is gladly lost; everything outside of Christ, however precious by human reckoning, is but refuse compared to him.

themes: I count all things but loss for the excellency of the knowledge of Christ Jesus my Lord
do count them but dung, that I may win Christ
counting all things but dung to win Christ
the surpassing worth of knowing Christ

schools.summary: Paul's deepest aim: to 'be found in him (in Christ, united to him, clothed in him)' — discovered, at the judgment and now, as one who is in Christ, not standing on his own merits; renouncing 'mine own righteousness, which is of the law (the righteousness of works, of legal observance, that one earns)' and embracing 'that which is through the faith of Christ, the righteousness which is of God by faith' — a righteousness not produced by self but given by God, received by faith in Christ; no part of justifying righteousness is the believer's own (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: this righteousness is imputed — Christ's righteousness reckoned to the believer, not his own; the believer is justified by a righteousness wholly outside himself, freely accounted to him by faith. The contrast of the righteousness 'of the law' and 'of God' overturns every scheme (the Papacy's included) that mingles works with grace as the ground of justification.

themes: not having mine own righteousness, which is of the law, but... the righteousness which is of God by faith
be found in him
the righteousness of God by faith, not of the law
imputed righteousness and justification by faith

schools.summary: Paul's aim in being found in Christ: 'that I may know him (experientially, intimately) and the power of his resurrection' — to know not facts about Christ but Christ himself, and the living power that raised him, working in the believer for new life; 'and the fellowship (koinonia — sharing, communion) of his sufferings' — to share in Christ's sufferings, to suffer with and for him, entering into communion with him through affliction (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: 'being made conformable unto his death' — dying to sin and self as Christ died, and willing even to share the manner of his death; the believer is shaped into Christ's likeness through suffering and dying, that he may also share his resurrection.

themes: That I may know him, and the power of his resurrection, and the fellowship of his sufferings
being made conformable unto his death
knowing Christ and the fellowship of his sufferings
conformed to his death

schools.summary: The goal of knowing Christ and sharing his death is 'if by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead' — the blessed resurrection of the just (exanastasis — the out-resurrection, the rising of believers to glory); 'if by any means' expressing not doubt of the fact (the resurrection is sure) but humility and earnest striving — the intensity of his pursuit and the sense of the goal's greatness, not uncertainty of attaining it (Barnes, Robertson, Wesley).

themes: If by any means I might attain unto the resurrection of the dead
Paul's aspiration to attain the resurrection
the resurrection of the just
earnest striving, not doubt

schools.summary: Paul disclaims having 'already attained' the prize, and presses on, pursuing it (the racing image: he keeps up the chase to lay hold of the prize for which Christ laid hold of him at his conversion). 'Neither were already perfect (tetelei0mai)' is Paul's denial that he has reached spiritual or moral perfection in this life — he is not yet made complete, free from sin; Barnes urges that one who claims a perfection Paul did not claim shows little knowledge of himself, and Robertson that Paul 'pointedly denies' any sudden absolute perfection attainable here. Some (the Pictorial Bible, Robinson) take teleioo in the racing sense of not yet having completed the course/won the prize.

Clarke: 'perfect' (teteleiomai) here is the athletic sense — Paul has not yet completed his course or received the crown (by martyrdom, the true consummation, teleiosis); the word says nothing of any deficiency in grace or indwelling sin, against those who 'falsely and dangerously' read it so. Wesley distinguishes one who is 'perfect' (fit for the race, v.15, strong in faith) from one 'perfected' (ready to receive the prize, glorified), which Paul is not yet; the conditional form implies not uncertainty but the difficulty of attaining.

debates.question: What does 'neither were already perfect' mean?

sides.position: Paul denies having attained sinless/moral perfection in this life
The racing sense — not yet having completed the course or won the crown (martyrdom); not about indwelling sin
Not yet 'perfected' (glorified, crowned), though there is a perfection (full maturity) attainable

themes: Not as though I had already attained, either were already perfect
I follow after, if that I may apprehend that for which also I am apprehended of Christ Jesus
Paul disclaiming attainment and pressing on
the crux of perfect

schools.summary: Paul repeats his humility: 'I count not myself to have apprehended (to have laid hold of the prize, to have arrived)'; 'but this one thing I do' — his whole energy focused on a single pursuit, the secret of his life concentration on one object; 'forgetting (leaving behind, not dwelling on) those things which are behind (past attainments and failures alike)' and 'reaching forth (epekteinomenos — stretching out, straining forward, like a runner with body bent toward the goal) unto those things which are before' (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: I count not myself to have apprehended: but this one thing I do
forgetting those things which are behind, and reaching forth unto those things which are before
single-minded forgetting the past and reaching forward
the one-pointed pursuit

schools.summary: The racing image completed: 'I press (run hard, pursue with all my might) toward the mark (skopos — the goal-post, the line that marks the finish)' — Paul drives toward the goal with concentrated, strenuous effort; 'for the prize (brabeion — the victor's reward, the crown) of the high (or upward) calling of God in Christ Jesus' — the heavenly reward to which God calls believers, the crown of eternal life and glory at the goal, the calling and prize alike coming from God through Christ (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: I press toward the mark for the prize of the high calling of God in Christ Jesus
pressing toward the mark for the prize
the high calling of God
the heavenly crown

schools.summary: 'As many as be perfect (teleioi)' here means the mature, the full-grown, the spiritually adult — not the absolute perfection denied in v.12 but relative perfection (Robertson: 'relative perfection, not the absolute... so pointedly denied in verse 12'; Clarke: thoroughly instructed, adults in Christian knowledge, as in 1 Corinthians 2:6; 14:20; Hebrews 5:14); let them 'be thus minded,' sharing Paul's mind of pressing on; and where any 'be otherwise minded,' God will in time 'reveal even this' — correcting and teaching the sincere. Ellicott adds a touch of irony, aimed at those who hold themselves 'already perfect,' bidding them prove it by a consciousness of their imperfection.

Wesley: 'perfect' here means those fit for the race, strong in faith (distinct from the 'perfected'/glorified of v.12); let such apply wholly to this one thing, and God, if they desire it, will reveal to the weaker what they do not yet see.

themes: Let us therefore, as many as be perfect, be thus minded
if in any thing ye be otherwise minded, God shall reveal even this unto you
the call for the mature to be thus minded
perfect meaning mature, not absolute

schools.summary: Paul's practical conclusion: 'whereto we have already attained (so far as we have advanced, in the truth we share) let us walk by the same rule (kanon — the same standard, line, or measure)' — believers keeping in step, ordering their lives by the truth they hold in common; 'let us mind (be intent on, set our minds on) the same thing' — united in aim and in the pursuit of the goal, not divided over the lesser matters on which they may yet differ; meanwhile living united in what they already know (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: whereto we have already attained, let us walk by the same rule, let us mind the same thing
the call to walk by the same rule
keeping in step by common truth
united in the shared while awaiting light

schools.summary: Paul's bold but humble charge: 'be followers together (symmimetai — joint-imitators) of me' — imitate me, as I imitate Christ (1 Corinthians 11:1); Paul offers his own life of pressing on as a pattern; and they are also to 'mark (observe, take note of, and follow) them which walk so as ye have us for an ensample (typos — pattern, model)' — those who live by the same example, the apostles and faithful believers being a model to be observed and copied (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).

Calvin: Paul could set himself forward as a pattern because his life conformed to Christ; the call is not to vanity but to point them to a living model of the gospel, that they may have a sure example to follow.

themes: Brethren, be followers together of me
mark them which walk so as ye have us for an ensample
the call to follow Paul's example
the pattern of the faithful

schools.summary: The contrast with the good example: 'many walk (live, conduct themselves)' in a way Paul has 'told you often, and now tell you even weeping' — living as 'enemies of the cross of Christ' (whether by their doctrine, rejecting the cross's offence like the Judaizers, or, more likely here, by their sensual, earthly-minded life, v.19, making the cross of no effect by their conduct); Paul speaks 'even weeping,' with tears, mourning over them rather than denouncing them coldly (Barnes, Robertson, Chrysostom).

themes: many walk... that they are the enemies of the cross of Christ
now tell you even weeping
Paul weeping over the enemies of the cross
grief over their ruin

schools.summary: The fourfold description of the enemies of the cross: 'whose end is destruction (apoleia — ruin, perdition)' — their final destiny everlasting ruin, the opposite of the salvation believers await; 'whose God is their belly' — they serve their appetites and sensual desires as their god, gluttony and self-gratification ruling them in God's place; 'whose glory is in their shame' — taking pride in what should disgrace them; 'who mind earthly things' — their whole thought and aim set on this world, not on heaven (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin).

themes: whose end is destruction, whose God is their belly, and whose glory is in their shame, who mind earthly things
the destiny and character of the enemies of the cross
serving the belly, minding earthly things
bound for destruction

schools.summary: The contrast with the earthly-minded: 'our conversation (politeuma — citizenship, commonwealth, the state to which we belong) is in heaven' — believers are citizens of heaven, their true home and allegiance there, not on earth; 'from whence (from heaven) also we look for (apekdechometha — eagerly await, expect) the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ' — believers wait for Christ's return from heaven, their hope set on his coming (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: while the enemies of the cross mind earthly things, the believer's heart and hope are in heaven, whence the Saviour will come; the heavenly citizenship governs the whole of the Christian's life and longing.

themes: For our conversation is in heaven
from whence also we look for the Saviour, the Lord Jesus Christ
the believer's citizenship in heaven
waiting for the Saviour from heaven

schools.summary: The returning Saviour 'shall change (transform, refashion) our vile body (the body of our humiliation — lowly, frail, mortal, subject to decay)' so that 'it may be fashioned like unto (conformed to) his glorious body (the body of his glory)' — made like Christ's own risen, glorified body; 'according to the working (energeia — the mighty operation) whereby he is able even to subdue (subject) all things unto himself' — the same almighty power by which Christ governs and will subdue all things refashioning the believer's body, his omnipotence guaranteeing the resurrection (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: the lowly body shall be conformed to the glorious body of Christ; the power that effects this is the same by which Christ subdues all things — so the believer's resurrection and glorification rest on Christ's omnipotence and are utterly sure.

themes: Who shall change our vile body, that it may be fashioned like unto his glorious body
according to the working whereby he is able even to subdue all things unto himself
the body transformed into Christ's glorious likeness
the resurrection by Christ's omnipotence
